original_abstract:
Abstract

Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors like BaTiO3 possess a remnant polarization that facilitates the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. This study reports the first instance of FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation using BaTiO3 nanocrystals. Tetragonal, ferroelectric nanocrystals were synthesized hydrothermally from TiO2 and barium hydroxide with a 63% yield. When deposited as films on tantalum substrates, these nanocrystals achieved water oxidation photocurrents of 0.141 mA cm⁻² at 1.23 V RHE under UV illumination. Application of an external electric field (52.8 kV cm⁻¹) to induce polarization resulted in a twofold increase or a 3.5-fold decrease in photocurrent, depending on the field's orientation. Furthermore, polarization shifted the onset potential by -0.15 V or +0.09 V and altered the surface photovoltage signal. Photocatalytic studies revealed that electric field exposure increased the H2 evolution rate of Pt/BaTiO3 by approximately 1.5 times and enhanced the selective photodeposition of silver onto the (001) crystal facets. All observed FE enhancements were reversible and could be neutralized by heating the material above its Curie temperature. These results indicate that FE dipoles modify the potential drop across the material's space charge layer, offering a viable mechanism for improving the efficiency of solar-to-fuel conversion systems.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract

Ferroelectric semiconductors such as BaTiO3 have a non-centrosymmetric structure with a permanent dipole moment (remnant polarization) which can help in separating photo-generated charges. Here we report, for the first time, that ferroelectric (FE) semiconductor BaTiO3 nanocrystals can not only enhance the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution reaction but can also be used as photocathodes and photoanodes for efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation. Tetragonal ferroelectric BaTiO3 nanocrystals were obtained using a hydrothermal synthesis from mixture of TiO2 and barium hydroxide with a product yield of 63%. Film growth solution Film formed BaTiO3 nanocrystals on tantalum substrate attained water oxidation photocurrent density of 0.141 mA cm−2 at an applied potential of 1.23 V versus RHE (vs RHE) under UV light illumination. At the applied electric field of 52.8 kV cm−1 (0.53 V or 165 mV across a diameter of 50 nm), the photocurrent was enhanced by two-fold and 3.5-fold with opposite direction of the applied electric field indicating the enhancement of PEC water oxidation performance by ferroelectric polarization switching and the shift of onset potential by 0.15 V or 0.09 V towards negative voltage and positive voltage, respectively. Also, the space charge photovoltage at the BaTiO3 surface was changed. With an external electric field applied to catalyst Pt/BaTiO3 (500 °C), the enhanced H2 evolution rates over Pt/BaTiO3 are obtained as about 1.5 times. FE also enhances selective photodeposition of silver at BaTiO3 (001) crystal facet. The FE effects in BaTiO3 nanocrystals are reversed with heating the samples above their Curie temperature. In summary, FE dipoles cause change in space charge potential in the space charge layer of FE BaTiO3 nanocrystals facilitating enhancement of photoelectrochemical and/or photocatalytic performance at the respective electrodes.

document.input:
Abstract

Ferroelectric (FE) semiconductors like BaTiO3 possess a remnant polarization that facilitates the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. This study reports the first instance of FE-enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation using BaTiO3 nanocrystals. Tetragonal, ferroelectric nanocrystals were synthesized hydrothermally from TiO2 and barium hydroxide with a 63% yield. Photocatalytic studies revealed that electric field exposure increased the H2 evolution rate of Pt/BaTiO3 by approximately 1.5 times and enhanced the selective photodeposition of silver onto the (001) crystal facets. All observed FE enhancements were reversible and could be neutralized by heating the material above its Curie temperature. These results indicate that FE dipoles modify the potential drop across the material's space charge layer, offering a viable mechanism for improving the efficiency of solar-to-fuel conversion systems.
